coredata.dc:title: Simultaneously enhanced strength and ductility of copper composites by tailoring the intragranular carbonized polymer dots distribution
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Rui
Luo, Haichuan
Liu, Liang
Yi, Jianhong
Tao, Jingmei
Li, Caiju
coredata.dc:description: Distribution states of reinforcement have considerable impact on the strength and ductility of metal composites. Herein, we reported a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy to simultaneously strengthen and stabilize Cu composites, which relied on a uniform dispersion of extremely fine carbonized polymer dots (CPD) inside grains. The intragranular CPD distribution not only elevated the strength of pure Cu by ∼45%, but also activated hardening mechanisms via dislocation-nanoparticle interactions. Meanwhile, 0.2 wt% CPD/Cu composite displayed good electrical conductivity of 96.5% IACS, exhibited a remarkable potential in applications as advanced multifunctional materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu composite
Carbonized polymer dots
Intragranular dispersion
Strain hardening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CPD and composite powders

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of fabrication process of CPD/Cu composite.
(a–b) TEM and HRTEM images of CPD, (c) statistical size distribution, (d) image of reduced CPD/Cu powders and corresponding EDS mappings for Cu, C and N.

Microstructure of 0.2 CPD/Cu composite characterized by (a) dark field TEM, (b–c) HRTEM of CPD-Cu interface and IFFT image of selected region in Fig. 3c; size distribution of CPD (d) inside grains, (e) at GBs.

(a–b) Tensile engineering stress-strain curves and strain-hardening rate versus true strain curves; (c) electrical conductivity of pure and composites with different CPD contents, (d) comparisons on the electrical conductivity and UTS of copper composites fabricated by different methods [23–27]; TEM images of (e–f) post-deformed CPD/Cu and (g) post-deformed CPD/Cu(BM) composite.
(a–b) Schematic illustration of microstructure, strengthening and toughening effects in CPD/Cu and CPD/Cu(BM), respectively.

head.ce:title: Simultaneously enhanced strength and ductility of copper composites by tailoring the intragranular carbonized polymer dots distribution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093 China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Distribution states of reinforcement have considerable impact on the strength and ductility of metal composites. Herein, we reported a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy to simultaneously strengthen and stabilize Cu composites, which relied on a uniform dispersion of extremely fine carbonized polymer dots (CPD) inside grains. The intragranular CPD distribution not only elevated the strength of pure Cu by ∼45%, but also activated hardening mechanisms via dislocation-nanoparticle interactions. Meanwhile, 0.2 wt% CPD/Cu composite displayed good electrical conductivity of 96.5% IACS, exhibited a remarkable potential in applications as advanced multifunctional materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu) and Cu alloys have been widely used in automotive and electronics fields due to their remarkable thermal conductivity and machinability [1,2]. However, the inferior stiffness, weak wear resistance and inadequate strength hindered their applications [3]. Although the alloying method can address partial limitations, it usually comes with severe sacrifice of electrical conductivity [4]. Recent researches demonstrated the intragranular reinforcement distribution together with robust interface bonding have great potential in breaking mechanical-physical incompatibly [5–7]. Thereinto, discontinuous particles reinforced Cu matrix composites, including TiC/Cu [8], BNNS/Cu [9], TiB2/Cu [10] and WC/Cu composites [11] have received widespread attention. However, these nano/micro sized particulates are commonly accustomed to being distributed along grain boundaries (GBs), giving rise to the intergranular embrittlement (due to composition segregation at GBs) [12,13]. In this regard, modifying the distribution states have tremendous influences on the properties. It remains challenging to achieve simultaneous improvements of strength, ductility and electrical conductivity in Cu composites.

Graphene, carbon nanotube (CNT), carbon nano-onion (CNO) etc. have been regarded as competitive reinforcements for next-generation composites [14,15]. Previous reports have revealed that short CNT are prone to dispersing inside grain interiors due to the small Zener pinning force and high mobility during thermal work processing [16,17]. Conversely, longer CNT prefer to locate at the GBs, which will completely remove the Orowan strengthening [18]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPD), so-called zero-dimensional carbon derivatives with an average size of ∼3 nm, possessing the characteristics of fluorescent colloid and displays uniform dispersion in aqueous solution [19,20]. Inspired by the above, we developed a new strategy to introduce intragranular nanocarbon in Cu grains by the molecular-level mixing (MLM) method. The “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy enhanced mechanical properties and electrical conductivity via incorporating high number density CPD into grain interiors. The substantially enhanced strength and uniform elongation of CPD/Cu composites were explored by revealing the relation between microstructure and dislocation-CPD interactions.

Initially, ethylenediamine and citric acid were combined in a hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for 6 h to produce CPD powder with average size of ∼2.68 nm. The synthetic method of CPD was described in our previous papers [19]. Intragranular dispersion of CPD was achieved by molecular-level mixing (MLM) method, detailed procedures were provided in Supplementary Material. The as-fabricated CPD/Cu powder was consolidated at 600 °C by spark plasma sintering (SPS) and obtained the corresponding CPD/Cu bulk composites. The cylindrical CPD/Cu were 20 mm in diameter and 5.5 mm in thickness. The schematic of experimental process was shown in Fig. 1 . For comparison, the intergranular dispersion of CPD was also fabricated by ball milling. The consolidated composite was denoted by CPD/Cu(BM) hereafter. The morphology and microstructure of powders and sintered bulks were researched by field emission electron microscope (FESEM, Nano Nova45) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 TF30 S-Twin). The tensile specimens were machined into the dog-bone shape with a gauge length of 10 mm, and a gauge cross-section of 2 mm × 1.2 mm. Tensile properties were measured at room temperature on a universal testing machine with strain rate of 5 × 10−4 s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CPD and composite powders
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the morphology of lab-synthesized CPD, the nanoparticles displays typical spherical characteristic with an average size of ∼2.68 nm. HRTEM exhibits definite crystal lattice with (010) interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, indicating high crystalline structure (in Fig. 2b). MLM-assisted dispersion strategy was used to enable the embedding of CPD inside grain interiors. As shown in Fig. 2d, the reduced CPD/Cu powders has a cubic-like shape, thereinto, CPD is hidden inside the grain interiors which hardly to be identified. Essentially, the hydrothermal reaction ensures Cu2+ and CPD mixing on the molecular level, promoting the heterogeneous nucleation of Cu2O through the cross-linking of functional groups. The consequence is a fine structured Cu grains with CPD distributed inside grains interior. EDS mappings represent the elements distribution of Cu and C, N atoms. C atoms has a uniform dispersion and remains the profile of cubic-like shape. Noting that the existed N atoms cannot be overlooked, which is introduced by the –NH2 and –NO3 on CPD. In our previous report, density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the existence of nitrogen doping is beneficial for the strong interfacial bonding, attributing to the enhanced binding energy and intense interaction with Cu atomic orbitals [21]. Such chemical bonding can suppress interfacial sliding of CPD inside grains.

Microstructural analysis of 0.2 CPD/Cu composite indicates that most CPD particles/clusters are on the nanoscale and homogeneously disperse in grain interior (in Fig. 3 a). The mean size of these nanoparticles is ∼16.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d. Besides, small fraction of CPD with an average size of ∼63 nm located at GBs (in Fig. 3(a, e)). It is assumed that the enclosed CPD particles isolated in Cu grains interior would hinder CPD aggregation and confine CPD clusters to finer nanometer scale. Thus, the intragranular CPD has smaller average size than those located at GBs. Coarser CPD clusters distributed along GBs tend to induce stress/strain concentration, which are prone to decohesion and cause intergranular fracture during loading [22]. The grains sizes of CPD/Cu and CPD/Cu(BM) reveal that the average grain size is ∼1.75 μm for the former, while ∼3.62 μm for the latter counterpart.

Normally, the incorporated nano-fillers would refine the grains by promoting grain nucleation and hindering grain growth. Compared with intergranular distribution, the dense intragranular CPD provides stronger resistance to grain coarsening and magnify grains refinement efficiency. The refined microstructure is benefit for driving down the concentration of deleterious segregation near GBs. HRTEM image is obtained through adjusting the incident zone axis of [
0
1 ‾
1
] for Cu crystal (in Fig. 3b). An individual CPD cluster can be determined by the typical feature, Cu-CPD interface is clean and no significant defects are present. Noting that a transitional intermixing Cu–C region forms in the vicinity of Cu-CPD interface (Fig. 3c), which originates from the enhanced interdiffusion between Cu and active C atoms from CPD. The intermixing region contributes to Cu-CPD interface by altering the mechanical adhesion into diffusion-assisted bonding [18]. Furthermore, it could alleviate the stress partitioning behavior near interface during early deformation, avoiding premature stress concentration.

Fig. 4 a shows the tensile curves of pure Cu and composites. Pure Cu displays yield strength (σy) of 225 ± 17 MPa, ultimate tensile strength (σu) of 252 ± 5 MPa, and failure strain (εf) of 22.5 ± 0.8%. For CPD/Cu composite, it exhibits simultaneously enhanced strength and ductility, the σu is ∼358 MPa and the εf is ∼25%. The improved ductility is attributed to the larger uniform elongation (εu), the value has increased from 14.8% to 17.3%. Additionally, the strength elevation of σy is significant (increased by +45%) in CPD/Cu composite, exerting by the majority of intragranular CPD dispersion. The strain-hardening responses, i.e., the variation of strain hardening rate (Θ) with true strain reveals that 0.2 CPD/Cu composite has the highest Θ during the whole deformation stage, as shown in Fig. 4(b). In comparison, for CPD/Cu(BM) composite, CPD predominantly distributed at GBs. Although it displays enhanced σu, it is compromised by the lack of work hardening and reduced εu. CPD forms large clusters and distribute along GBs. These clusters will induce stress concentration and interface debonding, which lead to lower Θ. Fig. 4c displays the variation tendency of electricity as function of CPD contents and distribution types. The electrical conductivity of 0.1% CPD/Cu is 97.8% IACS, which is almost equal to pure Cu. Meanwhile, CPD/Cu(BM) shows dramatic decrease tendency. Compared with other reported Cu composites [23–27], the highly competitive strength-conductivity synergy of CPD/Cu composite highlights the importance of intragranular CPD distribution for achieving superior properties.

The glorious properties arise from special microstructures, briefly, being associated with the intragranular dispersion CPD. Firstly, the high density of intragranular nanoparticles exert stronger resistance to grain coarsening and thus enhances the refinement strengthening. Secondly, compared with CPD/Cu(BM) composite, the enhanced strength and ductility is attributed to the higher Θ which originates from the generation and storage of dislocations without initiating cracks at GBs. As shown in Fig. 4e, TEM image shows the morphology of the post-deformed CPD/Cu, CPD still maintains the uniformly intragranular dispersion. Dislocation pinning and accumulation are clearly seen in Fig. 4f by adjusting the incident axis of z = [112]. One observes that multiple dislocations interact with intragranular CPD and are either generated around the particles or pinned by the particles. During plastic deformation, the retention of high dense dislocations inside grains interior gives rise to the significant work hardening. The dislocation storage ability in Cu grains can be considerably improved through this novel nanodispersion-in-grains strategy, leading to a remarkably enhanced strain-hardening capacity and a profound increase in uniform elongation. In comparison, CPD/Cu(BM) composite does not bode well for a large uniform elongation owing to the inability strain hardening. The dislocation accumulation inside grains is appreciably lower in the deformed CPD/Cu(BM) composite. Dislocation nucleated from GBs encounters limited number of obstacles, it travels across grain and is absorbed into an opposite GB, which leads to insignificant dislocation storage inside grains [5,28]. The vast majority of CPD distributes along GBs and forms coarser particles. Due to the deformation incompatibility, stress concentrations will be induced surrounding the particles, inducing interfacial decohesion, which eventually lead to the micro-cracks along GBs, as shown in Fig. 5 b. We further perform the molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the dislocation pinning and accumulation mechanisms associated with CPD, deep understanding of this section will be reported in the near future.

Based on the above analysis, strengthening and toughening behaviors of CPD nanoparticles with intragranular and intergranular distribution are schematically illustrated in Fig. 5. Compared with CPD/Cu(BM), the improved fine-grained strengthening, the intragranular CPD dispersion with smaller size would introduce more significant Orowan strengthening [29]. Besides, the high dispersion density of intragranular CPD would significantly promote the dislocation-reinforcement interactions and increase the dislocation storage capability (in Fig. 5a), being critical to higher strain hardening. Furthermore, the CPD-enhanced dislocation activities inside grains interior can benefit the accommodation of deformation compatibility among neighboring grains [12]. For the intragranular CPD dispersion, the strengthening efficiency and strain hardening capacity are greatly improved than those for precipitate phases in conventional aged Cu-based alloys, suggesting that CPD renders higher pinning resistance than crystalline precipitates.

As regards the remarkable electrical conductivity of CPD/Cu composite, an existence of additional electron transport pathway is implied in this microstructure, considering that the interfacial dislocations might predetermine a high electrical resistance. It can be rationalized by the local formation of conductive graphitic channels surrounding the tiny CPDs, and generates a strong electric field with negligible charge loss [24]. Additionally, the clean and close interface between Cu and CPD can minimize interfacial impedance stemming from electron-boundary scattering alongside the internal interfaces. Thus, the electrical conductivity of CPD/Cu is almost equal to pure Cu. However, coarser CPD clusters distributed along GBs tend to induce high electrical resistance and nonnegligible charge loss. Due to the severe electron-boundary scattering effect, CPD/Cu(BM) shows poor electrical conductivity.

In summary, this work represented an important step forward to conquer several mutually exclusive properties (mechanics and electricity) in Cu composites via a nanodispersion-in-grains strategy. The well-dispersed intragranular CPD had not only amplified the grain refinement efficiency but also activated strain hardening mechanisms via dislocation-nanoparticle interactions. The synergistic effect of robust Cu-CPD interface and dispersion strengthening of reinforcement rendered unique combinations of higher strength-ductility and good electrical conductivity in CPD/Cu composites.

Rui Bao: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Haichuan Luo: Writing – original draft, Validation. Liang Liu: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianhong Yi: Formal analysis, Visualization, Resources. Jingmei Tao: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Caiju Li: Formal analysis, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52174345, 52064032) and the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202002AB080001). Major Science and Technology Projects of Yunnan Province (Grant No. 202202AG050004).
